relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Isaac’s eyes were dim, and he called Esau close for the blessing.
He felt death near and wanted to bless before departing.
He sent Esau to the field with bow and quiver.
Food, love, appetite, and blessing became tangled together.
Rebekah was listening as Isaac spoke to Esau.
She turned quickly to Jacob with what she had heard.
The blessing was about to be given before the Eternal.
Rebekah commanded Jacob to listen to her voice.
She prepared a plan from the flock.
Jacob would bring the food and receive the blessing.
Jacob saw the physical problem: Esau was hairy, and he was smooth.
He feared being exposed and receiving curse instead of blessing.
Rebekah took the risk upon herself and pressed him to obey.
Jacob brought the goats, and Rebekah prepared what Isaac loved.
She clothed Jacob in Esau’s garments.
She covered his smoothness with skins.
The food and bread were placed in Jacob’s hands.
Jacob came to his father, and the question of identity opened: Who are you, my son?
Jacob claimed Esau’s name and firstborn place.
He even used the Divine name to explain the speed of the hunt.
Isaac sought truth by touch.
The voice was Jacob, but the hands were Esau.
Touch overruled voice, and Isaac blessed him.
The lie was confirmed one more time.
Isaac ate, drank, and prepared to bless.
The father asked for a kiss from the disguised son.
The smell of Esau’s garments became the smell of a blessed field.
Dew, earth’s richness, grain, and wine were spoken over Jacob.
Rule, service, curse, and blessing were sealed into the younger son’s future.
Jacob left just as Esau returned.
Esau brought the food meant to open his blessing.
Isaac asked again, Who are you? The true Esau stood before him too late.
Isaac trembled greatly, but the blessing remained.
Esau cried out bitterly for a blessing of his own.
The deception was named: Jacob had taken the blessing.
Esau heard Jacob’s name as destiny: heel-grasper, birthright-taker, blessing-taker.
Isaac could not simply undo what had been spoken.
Esau wept for the one blessing he still hoped might remain.
Isaac spoke a harder future over Esau.
Sword, service, and eventual breaking of the yoke became Esau’s path.
Hatred entered Esau’s heart, and he waited for mourning to become revenge.
Rebekah heard and moved again to preserve Jacob’s life.
She told him to flee to Laban in Haran.
She imagined anger cooling after a few days.
She hoped to bring him back before losing both sons.
She turned the danger into a marriage concern before Isaac, opening the way for Jacob’s departure.
